ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general naan bread entry (FOODON_03301709); no specific garlic naan variant exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers flat bread as a category; no garlic naan-specific code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for garlic naan or naan bread at time of processing.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard piece of lasuni naan weighs approximately 90 g, typical of a restaurant serving at an Indian restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Naan, Indian flat bread — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 revised edition), supplemented with USDA FoodData Central entry for Bread, naan, plain (FDC ID 175040) for micronutrient detail
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN provides per-100 g values for plain naan (maida-based, tandoor-baked). Garlic naan (lasuni naan) is a flavoured variant with added garlic, butter/ghee topping; macros are marginally adjusted upward for fat to reflect typical garlic-butter finish. USDA FDC 175040 used to cross-check and fill micronutrient fields. Values represent a composite best estimate for a standard restaurant-style garlic naan.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: The University of Sydney GI Database lists naan bread (white flour, tandoor-baked) with a GI of approximately 71 (high). Lasuni naan is a garlic-flavoured variant of plain naan; the addition of garlic and a small amount of butter does not materially alter the glycemic response of the wheat-flour base. GI of 71 applied from the closest analogous food (plain naan, white flour). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (71 × 52.1 × 0.90) / 100 ≈ 33.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~378 mg per standard 90 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals managing hypertension should limit lasuni naan to one piece per meal and avoid pairing it with other high-sodium dishes such as heavily salted curries or pickles, as the combined sodium load can exceed recommended single-meal limits of 600–800 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 71) and high glycemic load per serving (~33.4) due to refined wheat flour (maida) base
type2_diabetes.guidance: Lasuni naan is made from refined maida flour and carries a high GI of 71 and a high glycemic load per serving; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict portion size to half a piece, pair it with high-fibre legume-based dishes to blunt the postprandial glucose response, and consider whole-wheat naan alternatives where available.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from butter/ghee topping (~2.4 g per 100 g) and refined carbohydrate content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The garlic-butter finish on lasuni naan contributes saturated fat that can raise LDL cholesterol; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to one piece and request minimal butter topping, while favouring whole-grain alternatives to reduce the combined saturated fat and refined carbohydrate burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lasuni naan (also called garlic naan) is a popular leavened Indian flatbread made from refined wheat flour (maida), yogurt, yeast or baking powder, salt, and water, baked in a high-temperature tandoor clay oven and finished with a generous topping of minced or crushed garlic and melted butter or ghee. The word 'lasuni' (or 'lahsuni') derives from the Hindi/Urdu word for garlic ('lahsun'). The bread is soft, slightly chewy, and lightly charred on the surface, with a rich buttery garlic aroma. It is a staple accompaniment to North Indian curries, dals, and grilled meats, and is widely served in Indian restaurants across Singapore, including at establishments such as Kinara at Boat Quay. Nutritionally, lasuni naan is a moderate-energy, carbohydrate-dominant food (~297 kcal per 100 g, ~52 g carbohydrate per 100 g) with a meaningful protein contribution (~8.7 g per 100 g) from the wheat flour and yogurt. The fat content (~5.8 g per 100 g) is elevated relative to plain naan due to the butter or ghee topping. The glycemic index is high (GI ≈ 71), making portion control important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or insulin resistance. The sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g) warrants attention for those managing hypertension. Garlic provides trace amounts of vitamin C and allicin-related bioactive compounds, though these are largely diminished by high-heat baking. Lasuni naan is vegetarian and halal but contains gluten and dairy, making it unsuitable for those with coeliac disease or lactose intolerance.
common_user_queries: How many calories in garlic naan?
Is lasuni naan suitable for diabetics?
What is the GI of naan bread?
What is the difference between plain naan and garlic naan?
Can I eat naan if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No exact ICMR-NIN entry for garlic naan exists; values are adapted from the ICMR-NIN plain naan entry with a minor upward adjustment for fat to reflect the garlic-butter topping. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for plain white-flour naan. Overall nutritional profile is well-supported and internally consistent.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in either the ICMR-NIN or USDA FDC source entries for naan bread. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source; USDA FDC does not provide a reliable value for this preparation. All other -1 fields have been populated where defensible values were available from the composite source. Glycemic load per serving calculated using a 90 g serving size: GL = (71 × 52.1 × 0.90) / 100 = 33.4. The food_group has been reclassified from the input value of 'Mixed' to 'Grains and staples' as lasuni naan is a wheat-flour flatbread. Source cuisine tags 'Biryani' from the input are not relevant to this specific item and have been excluded from the output ontology and classification.

Populated all per_100g fields using ICMR-NIN adapted values; documented -1 sentinel values for vitamin_d_mcg and selenium_mcg with explicit notes.
